exits.label: ◂ take your leave — back to the Lamplit Quarter

npcs.name: Aldric Morn
npcs.title: the Doomguide who would tear down the Wall
npcs.hail: *(a tall, grey man sits at a small table where a kettle steams, and he does not rise, and he pours a second cup you will not drink and sets it before the empty chair, and only then looks up — and he already knows what you are)*

npcs.name: Wessa
npcs.title: the herald who set the kettle
npcs.hail: *(a young woman in a travel-stained tabard stands by the door with the wary stillness of someone guarding a man she half-worships and half-fears, and watches you the way you'd watch a lit fuse)*

npcs.name: a chill in the empty chair
npcs.title: seen by you alone
npcs.hail: *(there is a cold in the second chair that the steam of the untouched tea bends around, and as your dead-touched eyes adjust to the room you understand, with a lurch, that the chair is not empty — that a girl sits in it, patient, faded, watching her father pour tea she cannot drink, and that no one in this room can see her but you)*

variants.text: *(She stays planted by the door, and her hand does not leave the hilt at her belt, and her eyes are flat and unfriendly.)* …So *you're* the one he sent me down the causeway to fetch. *(quietly)* I'll be honest, since he won't let me be anything else under his roof: I didn't *want* to fetch you. Word came up the river that you've been *helping* the Choir — handing them the *marked*, balancing their *books.* *(her jaw tightens)* And now he wants you at his *table.* *(she shakes her head)* He sees something in you I don't, and that's — that's usually how it goes with him, and usually he's *right*, and I *hate* how usually he's right. *(she jerks her chin at the grey man)* Go on. Sit. Drink nothing. And mind — whatever you are to the *Choir*, in *here* you're a guest of the man who means to pull their whole *Wall* down. Pick a *side* before the *tea* goes cold.

*(The wary stillness cracks the moment she gets a clear look at you, and what replaces it is something close to awe she's trying very hard to fold back down.)* …It's *true*, then. *(under her breath)* You're the one. The causeway's full of it — a soul that walked up to the *Wall* and made it *flinch.* *(she straightens off the doorframe)* I carried the *summons* down myself, three days' ride, and I didn't half believe what I was *fetching.* *(she glances at the grey man at the table, and her voice drops, fierce and protective)* He's waited *twenty years* for someone like you. *Twenty.* So I'll say this once, fetcher to fetched: he is the *best* man I have ever served and he is doing a *terrible* thing for the *best* reason there is, and I do not know anymore which of those I'm *guarding.* *(she nods you toward the table)* …Just — be *careful* with him. He breaks easier than he looks. They *all* do, the ones who've decided to *carry* everything.

*(She doesn't move from the door, but she gives you a soldier's once-over, quick and thorough.)* You'll be the one the kettle's for. *(flatly)* I'm Wessa. I carry his words up and down the causeway, and I fetch the people he wants fetched, and I do *not*, as a rule, *like* most of them. *(she studies you)* He's a *great* man, the one at that table. Greatest I've served. And 'great men' are how you spell *'about to ask you for something enormous'* in my experience. *(she nods you in)* Sit. Hear him out. He's *earned* the hearing, whatever he's about to ask. *(quieter)* Just don't let the *quiet* voice and the *good* tea fool you. He'd burn this whole *city* to do what he means to do, and he'd *weep* while it burned, and he'd do it *anyway.* That's the part nobody warns you about. So I'm warning you.

nodes.speaker: Wessa
nodes.text: *(She keeps her post by the door, arms crossed, the reluctant doorkeeper of a man she cannot decide whether to protect from the world or the world from him.)* Ask, if you're asking. *(she tips her head)* I'd rather you got your questions out *here*, with *me*, than waste *his* time — he's not got much of it, and he spends what he has on the *count*, not on *small talk.*

choices.text: "Who is he, Wessa? Truly. The causeway says a dozen things — a heretic, a saint, a madman with a kettle. Which did you sign on to serve?"

[INSIGHT] "You keep looking at the empty chair. Not at him — at the *chair.* You set a cup there yourself, didn't you, the first time, before you knew better. Who's supposed to be sitting in it, Wessa?"
"You said he'd burn the city and weep and do it anyway. That's not a warning about *him*, Wessa. That's a warning about *you* — about what you're afraid you'll *let* him do. Say it."

*(let the herald feel the cold that came in with you)* "You half-worship him for hating the Wall. I've been *inside* the Wall, Wessa — I came back wearing it. So you can stop wondering what I am. I'm the thing your master has prayed twenty years to meet. Take me to his table."
"I've heard enough. Let me speak to him myself."

nodes.speaker: Wessa
nodes.text: *(She's quiet a moment, choosing.)* …Aldric Morn. *(she says the name like it weighs something)* Twenty years ago, the finest Doomguide of Kelemvor on the whole Sword Coast — the one they *sent* for the hard deaths, the *plagues*, the *battlefields*, because he could stand in the worst of it and make every soul's passing *gentle* and *fair.* *(her voice roughens)* He *believed* it. The Judge weighs all souls fairly. He gave his whole *life* to it. *(she looks at the grey man)* And then the Judge's own *Wall* took someone he loved — *unjudged*, *unweighed*, for the *crime* of having no god — and the man who spent forty years trusting the *fairness* of death watched it be *monstrously* unfair to the one soul he'd have died to protect. *(quietly)* So now he means to *depose* the god he served. Tear the Wall down. Free every unclaimed soul in it. *(she meets your eyes)* Heretic, saint, or madman — I signed on because a man who'd *unmake a god* for *love* is the only kind of *great* I've ever found worth following. I just didn't know, then, what it would *cost* to follow him. I'm *learning.*

nodes.speaker: Wessa
nodes.text: *(She flinches, and her crossed arms tighten, and for a second the soldier is just a young woman who walked into something too large for her.)* …You *saw* that. *(low)* The first night I served him, I set the table for *two.* Habit — you set for the *household.* And he — *(she swallows)* — he didn't *correct* me. He just *poured* the second cup, like he does *every* night, for the chair *nobody sits in*, and I realised the *household* was *one man and an empty chair*, and I have not set that table *wrong* since, because *watching* him pour it *right* is *worse.* *(she looks at the chair, and away)* I never *asked* who. You don't *ask.* But it's a *daughter's* chair, I'd stake my *sword* on it — there's a particular *way* a man grieves a *child*, and he's got *all* of it. *(quietly)* The Wall took someone of his, the stories say. I think the someone *sat there.* I think he *pours* for her because it's the only place left he's *allowed* to. The Wall didn't even leave him a *grave* to kneel at. Just — *(she gestures, helpless)* — a *chair.*

nodes.speaker: Wessa
nodes.text: *(The arms come uncrossed. She looks at you a long, hard moment, and then — because you've named it, and naming it is a relief she didn't know she needed — she lets it out, low and fast.)* …Yeah. *(rough)* Yeah, all right. It's *me.* *(she glances at Aldric, makes sure he's out of earshot)* He's going to ask me, one day, to do something I *shouldn't.* Burn a village that's *over* the harvest's source. Spend a *town* to crack the *Wall.* And the *terrible* thing — the thing that wakes me — is that when he asks, in that *quiet* voice, with that *true* grief in it, with the *count* in his eyes — *(her voice cracks)* — I think I'll *do* it. I think I'll *light* the *match.* Because he'll be *right*, in the *enormous* way he's *always* right, and being *right* about the *ends* is how *good* people end up doing the *unforgivable.* *(she grips her own elbow)* So I watch the *chair*, and I watch *him*, and I keep *waiting* to find the line I *won't* cross — *(she looks at you)* — and I'm *terrified* I'm going to meet it the *day* I cross it, and not a *moment* before. *(quietly)* Maybe *you're* the line. The *Returned* who can do what he *couldn't.* Maybe you're how he *unmakes* the Wall *without* the *matches.* *Gods*, I *hope* you are. I'm so *tired* of bracing for the *fire.*

nodes.speaker: Wessa
nodes.text: *(She gives you a flat look and steps back into her doorkeeper's stance, shutters down.)* You're *fishing.* *(dryly)* I can tell — I do it myself, to people I'm sizing up. *(she folds her arms)* I'm a *herald*, friend, not a *keyhole.* What I know about the man at that table I learned by *serving* him, and it's not for *trading* to a stranger who's *guessing* at the *furniture.* *(she nods at Aldric)* You want to *read* somebody, *read* him. He's the one with the *answers* — and the one with the *questions* for *you.* I just *open* the door and *worry.* It's a full-time post, the *worrying.* Go on in.

nodes.speaker: Wessa
nodes.text: *(She goes very still, and then — slowly — she steps *aside* from the door, and there is something in her face that is equal parts dread and an enormous, painful, dawning *hope.*)* …You're *Returned.* *(barely)* Then it's — he wasn't *mad.* All these years, the thing he *swore* must exist, the soul that could go *into* the Wall and *out* — the *living proof* his cause wasn't *grief* dressed as *strategy* — *(she presses a hand to her mouth)* — it's *standing in his doorway.* *(she looks at the grey man at the table, and her eyes are wet)* He is going to look at you, fetcher, and he is going to see *twenty years of being right* walk through the door, and I do not *know* what that will *do* to a man who has held himself *together* on *being right* this *long.* *(fiercely, quietly)* So be — be *gentle*, or be *honest*, but do not be *cruel* to him. Whatever he's *become*, he *earned* it the worst way there is. *(she opens the way fully)* Go. He's waited longer for *you* than he's waited for *anything.* Longer than he waited for his god to be *fair.* Don't make him wait through the *tea* going cold.

nodes.speaker: Wessa
nodes.text: *(She steps aside, the wary doorkeeper giving way.)* Aye. He's the one you came for. *(she nods at the table)* I just *open* the door. *(quietly, as you pass)* …Whatever he asks you for — and he *will* ask, that's the *whole* of why the kettle's on — remember it's *his* count, not *yours.* He's been adding to it for *twenty* years. Don't let him *start* you one. *(a beat, low)* It's the *heaviest* arithmetic there is. I've watched it *bend* a great man double. Don't pick up the *pencil.*

conversations.title: The Chill in the Empty Chair
conversations.npc: a chill in the empty chair

variants.text: *(She does not startle when your eyes find her — she has been waiting, you understand, for someone whose eyes *could.* She is perhaps nineteen, faded at the edges like a letter left in the sun, and she sits with her hands folded in her lap, watching her father pour the tea, and when she finally speaks her voice reaches you from very far away and very close at once.)* …You can *see* me. *(not a question; a held breath finally let go)* Oh. *Oh.* It's been — I don't *know* how long. He sets the cup. Every night. And I *sit* here, and I *watch* him not-drink-it across from me, and I *talk*, and talk, and *talk*, and he never — *(her faded eyes brim)* — you're the *first* one. In all this time. The first one who came in and *looked at the chair* and *saw the girl in it.* *(she leans forward, urgent, weightless)* Please. *Please* don't pretend you can't. I will *un-fade* a little, just to be *seen* — I can feel it already, you looking, it's like *warmth* — *(a wet, weightless laugh)* — gods, I'd forgotten *warmth.* Stay. *Talk* to me. I have been so *unbearably* alone in the one chair in the world I'm *not* alone in.)*

nodes.speaker: the girl in the chair
nodes.text: *(She watches you with the desperate, careful hope of someone who has learned not to want things, trying to want this one carefully enough that it won't be taken.)* I'm — *(she pauses, as if the name takes effort to lift)* — Eithne. I *was* Eithne. I'm his *daughter.* *(she glances at the grey man, fond and unbearably sad)* He thinks he pours the cup for a *memory.* He doesn't know I'm *here.* He doesn't know I *followed him home* from the Wall. *(she turns back to you)* Ask. Anything. It has been *years* since a question was *for* me.

choices.text: "You followed him home from the Wall. Then you're not *in* it — not all of you. How, Eithne? The Faithless don't get to *leave.*"
"He's going to tear the whole Wall down for you, Eithne. Burn whatever it takes. Do you *want* that — to be the reason a man unmakes a god and a city with it?"

[INSIGHT] "You're fading, and you're frightened of it — but that's not the fear in you, is it. The fear is that if he ever *succeeds*, if he tears it all down and you're finally free — you'll have to tell him it didn't have to cost what he paid. That he became a monster for a daughter who'd rather he'd just *grieved.*"

*(one of the Returned to one of the held)* "I came back, Eithne. All the way out. I don't know yet if I can take anyone *with* me — but I'm learning, soul by soul, and I am *not* going to leave you in a chair watching your father drown. Hold on. You're on my list now, and my list is the one thing the Wall is starting to fear."
"Does he need to know I can see you?"

nodes.speaker: the girl in the chair
nodes.text: *(She looks down at her faded hands, turning them over.)* …Most of me *is* in it. *(quietly)* The Wall has the — the *weight* of me. The *years* I won't get. The *warmth.* *(she looks up)* But when they mortared me in, my father was *there* — he'd *come*, he'd *fought* his way to the causeway to *stop* it and he was too *late*, and the last thing I *saw*, going into the cold, was his *face.* *(her voice trembles)* And I think — I think a soul that goes into the dark *looking* at the one it loves most leaves a *thread* behind. A *little* of itself, snagged on the *living* one. *(she gestures at the room, the cup, the grieving man)* So *this* much of me — the *looking* part, the part that couldn't stop *watching* him — it *followed the thread home.* And here I *sit.* Not *alive.* Not *gone.* Just… *looking.* Forever. At a man who pours me tea and cannot *see* that the *looking* worked. *(a faded, breaking smile)* It's the cruelest and kindest thing that's ever happened to me, being snagged on my father's *grief.* I get to *stay.* I just don't get to be *seen.* …Until *you.*

nodes.speaker: the girl in the chair
nodes.text: *(Her face crumples, and the fading edges of her seem to flicker with the force of it.)* …*No.* *(fierce, weightless)* No, I don't *want* it. *(she looks at her father with twenty years of helpless love)* Do you think I *haven't* tried to *tell* him? I sit *here*, every night, *screaming* it into a face that can't *hear* — *'Papa, stop, I'm not worth a* city, *I was never worth a* village, *put it* down*, just* grieve *me like a* normal *dead girl and let me* go—' *(her voice breaks)* — and he *pours the tea*, and *plans the burning*, and calls it *love*, and it *is* love, that's the *horror*, it's the *purest* love there ever was and it's going to *kill thousands* and I am the *reason* and I *can't make him hear me.* *(she turns to you, desperate)* But *you* can be heard. *You* he'll listen to — the *Returned*, the *proof.* *(pleading)* When he asks you to *help* him tear it down with *fire* — tell him *no.* Tell him there's a *better* way, a *gentler* way, *any* way that doesn't end with *more* daughters in *more* walls. *(weightless tears)* Don't let my father *become the Wall* trying to *break* it. That's the only thing I've *wanted* for *twenty years*, and I've never had a *mouth* he could *hear* — until *you* walked in.

nodes.speaker: the girl in the chair
nodes.text: *(She goes utterly still, and for a moment the fading stops, as though the truth of it has fixed her briefly more *real.*)* …You see *all* the way in. *(a whisper)* Yes. That's the *one* I can't even say to *myself*, most nights. *(she looks at her father)* I'm not afraid of *fading.* Fading's *rest.* I'm afraid of him *winning.* *(the words come hard)* Because if he *tears it down* — if he pays the *whole* monstrous price and *frees* me — then I have to *float* up to him, *finally* seen, *finally* free, and watch his *face* when he realises… *(she can barely say it)* …that I'd have rather *dissolved.* That I'd have rather been *nothing* than be the *reason* for the *count.* That every soul he *spent* to *save* me, I'd have handed *back* in a *heartbeat* to have my *Papa* be a man who *grieved his girl* instead of a man who *unmade the world* for her. *(weightless, devastated)* He thinks he's doing it *for* me. And the truest thing I know, the thing that would *break* him, is that the *daughter* he's *burning the world* for has been sitting in this chair for *twenty years* wishing he'd *let her go.* *(she looks at you)* How do you *tell* a man that? That his *love* is the *cruelest* thing that ever happened to *both* of you? …Maybe you don't. Maybe that's *my* burden, and the kindest thing you can do is help him *stop* before he ever has to *hear* it.

nodes.speaker: the girl in the chair
nodes.text: *(She gives you a faded, gentle smile, and looks back at her father, and something in her closes softly, like a hand over a candle.)* …You're *kind* to ask. *(quietly)* But some things a *ghost* keeps even from the one soul who can *hear* her. *(she folds her weightless hands)* I've had *twenty years* to learn what's *safe* to *want* out loud, and the answer is *very little.* Wanting things is how the *fading* finds the *soft* parts. *(she watches the steam bend around her)* Just — *stay* a moment. Don't *ask*, only *stay.* Let me be *seen* a little longer. That's enough. That's *more* than I've had since the cold closed over. *(a whisper)* The *seeing* is the warmest thing. Warmer than the *fire.* Warmer than the *tea* he pours and pours and I can never quite *reach.*

nodes.speaker: the girl in the chair
nodes.text: *(The faded edges of her *flare* — for one instant she is almost solid, almost a living girl, lit from inside by something she had clearly buried as too dangerous to feel.)* …Your *list.* *(a weightless sob)* I'm on your *list.* *(she presses both hands to her mouth)* I stopped — I made myself *stop* hoping, years ago, because hope is *teeth*, hope *finds* the soft parts and the *fading* eats them — but you said it so *plainly*, like a *fact*, like *weather*: 'I'm learning. Hold on.' *(she looks at you, fierce and weightless and alive with it)* Then I'll *hold.* I'm *good* at holding — I've held onto *one thread* of myself for *twenty years* just to *watch my father*, I can *hold* onto *you.* *(a breath)* But — *(she glances at the grey man, and her face goes complicated)* — when you free me, Returned. *If* you can. *Promise* me it won't be the way *he* means to do it. *No* city. *No* count. *No* more daughters in walls to buy *this* one out. *(fierce)* I would rather *fade* than be *bought* with someone *else's* Eithne. *Promise* me a *gentle* door, or leave me in the *chair.* I've grown — *(weightless, wry)* — I've grown *fond* of the chair. It has a *view* of him. That's not *nothing.* That's, gods help me, very nearly *enough.*

nodes.speaker: the girl in the chair
nodes.text: *(Her faded face fills with a fear sharper than anything the Wall put there.)* …*No.* *(fast, pleading)* No — not *yet*, not — *(she stops, forces herself calmer)* …I don't *know.* That's the *honest* answer. *(she watches her father)* For *twenty years* I've *wanted* nothing but for him to *know* I'm *here* — to *turn*, and *see* me, and stop pouring tea into the *silence.* *(her voice trembles)* But if you *tell* him… *(she shakes her head)* He's holding himself *together* with the *count* and the *cause* and the *certainty* that I'm *gone* and only *vengeance* is *left.* And if you *hand* him *proof* I'm *still here*, still *watching*, still — *(she swallows)* — I don't know if it would *save* him or *shatter* him. A man can survive *grief.* I'm not sure he can survive *hope* this *late*, after what he's already *paid* for it. *(she looks at you, torn)* So… *carry* it. *Carefully.* Know that I'm here. And tell him *only* if you find a moment where it would be a *door* for him, and not a *cliff.* *(quietly)* You'll know it if it comes. You're the sort who *would.* That's why you're the only one who can *see* me. The Wall lets the *gentle* ones see the most. It's how it *hurts* us *best.*

variants.text: *(He pours the second cup for the empty chair before he pours yours — that order, you sense, is not negotiable — and when he finally looks up, his grey eyes have the particular weight of a man who has just had his life's work confirmed by a stranger.)* …Sit. *(quietly)* You've come from the counting-house. I can see it on you — the *Canon's* certainty, *cracked*, all over your hands like *plaster dust.* *(he sets down the kettle)* Do you understand what you *did* in there? In *one afternoon*, you turned a *High Measurer* of the Choir — a thing I have been trying to do, by *fire* and *fear* and *twenty years* of patient ruin, and *never once* managed. *(he studies you with something close to awe and close to fear)* You did it with *words.* And a *crack* in a *ledger.* *(he leans forward)* I have spent two decades certain the Wall could only come down the *hard* way. The *expensive* way. The way that *costs* villages. *(softly)* And then *you* walk up my causeway and start taking it apart with *mercy*, and I find I do not know whether to *weep*, or *recruit* you, or be *afraid* that you've come to show an old monster he chose the *butcher's* road for *nothing.*

*(He pours the cup for the empty chair, then yours, and when he looks up there is no warmth in it — only a deep, grieved disappointment, which is somehow worse than anger.)* …Sit. *(quietly)* I sent for you because the causeway said a *Returned* had come — the soul I have *prayed* twenty years to meet. *(he sets down the kettle)* And then the rest of the word came up the river behind you. That you've been *helping* them. Handing the Choir its *marked.* Balancing the *books* of the very *machine* I have given my *life* to *break.* *(his jaw works)* I do not *understand* you. A soul that *walked out of the Wall* — that knows *first-hand* what it *is* — choosing to *feed* it. *(he meets your eyes, and his are terrible and sad)* So before I decide what you *are* to me — *ally*, or *obstacle*, or the bitterest *enemy* I have ever been handed — *tell* me, Returned. How does a soul that *escaped* the cold turn *around* and *post* others *into* it? I would *genuinely* like to know. I have *killed* for less understanding than I'm offering *you* right now.

*(He marks the grey balance at your throat, and a complicated thing crosses his face — recognition, grief, the ghost of a brotherhood he renounced.)* …A servant of the Judge. *(quietly, pouring the empty chair's cup)* I wore that sigil for *forty years.* The finest in the order, they said — the one you *sent* for the hard deaths. *(he sets it down)* I *believed* it, every word: that the Judge weighs all souls *fairly*, that death is the one place the *poor* and the *godless* and the *unlucky* finally get a *fair* reckoning. *(his voice goes flat and cold)* And then I watched His *Wall* take a soul who *deserved* fairness more than anyone I ever *guided*, and give her *nothing* — no judgement, no weighing, no *door* — just the *slow* dissolve of the *unclaimed.* *(he looks at you)* So you and I are *brother* and *sister* in a *broken* faith, Judge's-servant. The difference is that I *stopped* lying to myself about what we *serve.* Sit. Let me show you what's on the *other* side of that grey balance, once you stop *flinching* from it.

*(He pours a second cup for the empty chair — carefully, the way you'd pour for an honored guest — and then a third for you, and only then does he raise his grey eyes, and they are the eyes of a man who has not slept fully in twenty years and has decided that is the correct number.)* Sit. *(quietly)* The kettle's on; the least you can do is let it have been worth boiling. *(he gestures at the chair)* You'll forgive an old man his rituals. The tea, the chairs, the *count* — they're the scaffolding I use to keep standing. *(he studies you, and something in him sharpens)* I sent for you because the whole causeway has gone *strange* this week, and every strange thing in it traces back to *you.* A marked girl, talked off the water. A clerk, broken. A High Measurer, *doubting.* *(softly)* The Choir does not *doubt*, Returned. I have spent *twenty years* failing to make it doubt. And you've done it in a *week*, on *foot*, with *words.* *(he folds his hands)* So. I should *very* much like to know what you are, and what you *want* — because if it is *anything* like what *I* want, then you may be the answer to a prayer I stopped *believing* I'd ever have answered. Drink, or don't. But *talk.*

nodes.speaker: Aldric Morn
nodes.text: *(He wraps both hands around his own untouched cup — for the warmth, you realise, not the tea; he never drinks it either — and waits, with the bottomless patience of a man who has learned that everything worth having takes longer than a life.)* Ask me what you came to ask. *(quietly)* I have answered to *kings*, to *Justiciars*, to my own *god* before I left him. I will answer to a *Returned* gladly. You are the first guest at this table in twenty years who might *understand* the answers.

choices.text: "The whole causeway says you'd burn cities to break the Wall. Is it true, Aldric? And if it is — how do you square 'free every soul' with 'spend whoever's standing nearby'? That's the Choir's own arithmetic, just pointed the other way."

[INSIGHT] "You pour for the empty chair first. Every time. Before your own cup, before your guest's. Whoever sat there isn't a *memory* to you, Aldric — she's the *only* guest. The cause, the count, the Crown — it's all just the long way around setting one more cup for one more night. Who was she?"

[RELIGION] "You mean to depose Kelemvor — to take the Crown of Horns and unmake the god of the dead. You understand that doesn't *free* the souls, Aldric. It *unmoors* them. Kill the Judge and there's no court at all, just the Wall and the Unmade and ten thousand souls with nowhere left to even *appeal.* You'd not be breaking the machine. You'd be *removing the last mercy in it.*"

*(tell him what you did to his enemy with mercy instead of fire)* "I cracked your High Measurer this week, Aldric — not with a sword, with an *argument.* The appeal. The unpicking. The Canon herself is going to widen the crack from the *inside.* The Wall can come down *gently.* You don't need the matches. You never did — you just couldn't *see* the gentle road from inside your own grief."

*(let one who has been in the Wall speak to the one who'd break it)* "You've spent twenty years imagining what the Wall is. I don't have to imagine, Aldric. I've been *in* it. So before you spend one more village on it, hear it from the only soul who came back: I'll tell you what's actually in that stone — and what it would actually take to empty it. The truth is worse than your nightmares, and more hopeful. Both."
"Tell me about the Crown of Horns. What it is. What it costs. What you become if you take it."

nodes.speaker: Aldric Morn
nodes.text: *(He does not flinch from it. He turns his cup a slow half-circle and meets your eyes with the terrible candour of a man who worked this out long ago and chose it anyway.)* …Yes. *(quietly)* It's true. And yes — it is the Choir's arithmetic, pointed the other way. *(he nods slowly)* You think I haven't *seen* that? I keep a *count*, Returned. Every soul already dead for this, every soul still to die. I add to it whether I light the match or not — the spread is *mine*, the harvest is *mine.* *(his jaw tightens)* The Choir spends the *unbought* to keep the world *orderly.* I spend whoever I *must* to make the world *just.* And you're right that the *spending* looks the same from the *outside* — a village is a village; it burns the same for a *good* cause as a *wicked* one. *(he leans forward, and there is twenty years of grief and iron in it)* But here is the difference, and it is the *only* one I have left to stand on: the Choir spends souls to keep the Wall *running.* I spend them to bring it *down.* One arithmetic *feeds* the horror *forever.* The other *ends* it, and then the *spending stops.* *(quietly)* I am not a *good* man anymore, Returned. I gave that up at a wall I came to too late. I am an *arithmetic* with a *kettle.* But it is arithmetic in the service of the *last* subtraction — the one that makes all the *others* stop. *(he searches your face)* …Unless you've come to tell me my sums are *wrong.* Gods know I've prayed for *someone* to check them. I have not *dared* to, myself, in *years.* I'm *afraid* of what I'd *find.*

nodes.speaker: Aldric Morn
nodes.text: *(The cup goes still in his hands. For a long moment he does not breathe, and the only sound is the kettle's small complaint, and when he speaks his voice has lost every ounce of its iron and is just — a father's.)* …You see clearly. *(barely)* Eithne. *(he says the name like it is the last warm thing he owns)* My daughter. *(he looks at the empty chair, and his composure does not break so much as *erode*, grain by grain.)* She had no god. My own *child*, raised in the house of the Judge's *finest servant*, and she looked at all of it — the weighing, the fairness, the doctrine I'd given my *life* to — and she said, *'Papa, I can't pretend to a faith I don't feel just to be safe when I die. That would be the one true lie, and you raised me not to tell those.'* *(his voice cracks)* I was so *proud* of her. *Gods*, I was proud. My honest girl. *(a long, terrible pause)* And then she *died* — a fever, nothing grand, nothing she *chose* — and because she would not *lie* about a *god*, the Wall took her *unclaimed*, and I got to the causeway in time to watch the *cold* close over my honest girl's *face*, and I have set her a cup *every night since*, because the Wall did not even leave me a *grave.* *(he looks at you, hollow)* You ask why I'd burn cities. *That's* why. Not for *vengeance.* For the *next* honest girl. So that *no* father ever again pours tea for a *chair.* *(quietly)* You're the first soul in twenty years to ask her *name* instead of my *plan.* …Thank you. It is good to *say* it. *Eithne.* She was *real.* The cup is not for *nothing.*

nodes.speaker: Aldric Morn
nodes.text: *(His hand tightens on the cup, and the iron comes down over his face like a visor.)* …You reach for the *chair.* *(flatly)* Everyone does, eventually. It is the most *interesting* thing in a spare room. *(he does not look at it)* It is a *chair*, Returned. I keep an *empty* place because a man who lives *alone* with a *cause* needs to remember he was once a man who lived *with* someone. That is *all.* *(but his knuckles are white)* You did not come here to *furnish* my *grief.* You came because the *causeway* sent you, or because *I* did, or because the Wall has its hooks in *you* the way it has them in *me.* *(coldly)* Ask me about *that.* The chair is not *for sale*, and it is not for *reading.* Some accounts a man keeps *closed* even from the *Returned.*

nodes.speaker: Aldric Morn
nodes.text: *(He goes very still, and the candour in his face curdles into something between fury and dread — the look of a man hearing the one objection he has spent two decades refusing to total.)* …'Remove the last mercy in it.' *(quietly, dangerously)* You think I haven't *lain awake* on that? *(he sets the cup down with great care)* You're not *wrong* that killing the Judge unmoors the court. I *know* what the Crown does — I have read every *fragment*, chased every *heresy*, paid in *blood* for the knowledge. Take the Crown of Horns and you do not *reform* the realm of the dead. You *replace* its *king.* *(his voice drops)* And here is the part I do not say to *Wessa*, or to my *allies*, or to the *empty chair*: I am not *certain* I would stop at *tearing the Wall down.* A soul that holds the Crown could *unmake* the Wall, yes — but it could also *unmake the rule that built it.* It could sit in the Judge's seat and decree that *no* soul is ever *unclaimed* again, that the *fairness* I believed in *becomes true* because *I* would *make* it so. *(he looks at you, and it is the most frightened and most honest he has been)* Do you hear what I just *said?* I started out to *free* the dead. And somewhere in twenty years I began, very quietly, to plan on becoming a *god* who would do it *right.* *(a whisper)* *That* is what the Wall does, Returned. It doesn't just *kill* the godless. It turns the ones who *loved* them into *exactly* the kind of *absolute* power that *built* it. You've caught me one *honest* question short of *apotheosis.* …Ask the *next* one carefully. I am closer to the *edge* than even *I* knew until you *said* it.

nodes.speaker: Aldric Morn
nodes.text: *(He hears your theology out, and shakes his head, not unkindly, the patience of a man who has had this argument with cleverer heretics than you.)* A *neat* objection. *(quietly)* 'Kill the Judge and there's no court.' The *seminary* answer, and the *Justiciars* throw it at me every time they try to *talk* me down before they try to *kill* me. *(he turns his cup)* But you've *skipped* a step, Returned. I do not mean to *kill* the Judge and leave the *seat empty.* I mean to *take* it. The Crown of Horns is not a *weapon.* It is a *throne.* *(he meets your eyes)* Bring me a *sharper* objection — one that accounts for a *new* hand on the *scales*, and not merely an *empty* bench — and I will *weigh* it, gladly. But 'you'd remove the last mercy' assumes I mean to leave *mercy* with *no one holding it.* *(coldly)* I mean to *hold* it *myself.* …That should frighten you *more* than the *burning.* If it doesn't, you haven't understood what I'm *asking* for.

nodes.speaker: Aldric Morn
nodes.text: *(He listens — and as you speak, you watch something happen to Aldric Morn that you suspect has not happened in twenty years: hope, arriving so late and so unlooked-for that it lands like a *blow.* He grips the table's edge.)* …*Gently.* *(barely)* The Canon. *Doubting.* The appeal. *Widening the crack from the inside.* *(he stares at you)* You are telling me there is a road where the Wall comes down and the *villages do not burn.* *(his voice shakes)* I stopped *looking* for that road, Returned. Twenty years ago. I decided it did not *exist* — that *anyone* who claimed it did was *soft*, or *lying*, or had not *stood at the causeway* and *watched* — and I *built* my whole *self* around the *certainty* that the only way out was *through the fire.* *(he presses a hand over his eyes)* And you found it in a *week.* Standing in front of a *Canon*, with *words.* *(when he lowers his hand, his grey eyes are wet, and twenty years younger, and *terrified* of how much he wants to believe you)* Do you understand what you may have just *done* to me? If you are *right* — if the gentle road is *real* — then every village on my *count*, every soul I *spent*, every year I spent *becoming this* — *(his voice breaks)* — was *unnecessary.* I made myself a *monster* for a *door that was unlocked the whole time.* *(a long, shaking breath)* …I have to know if you're *right.* I have to. Even if knowing *damns* everything I've already *done.* *Show* me the gentle road, Returned. And if it holds — gods help me — I will *put down the matches*, and I will spend whatever is *left* of me *walking* it instead. I did not think I had a *choice* anymore. You have just handed me the most *terrible* gift there is: the *possibility* that I did.

nodes.speaker: Aldric Morn
nodes.text: *(He sets down his cup. He leans forward. And for the first time the grief and the iron both fall away, and underneath is the thing that has driven all of it: a father, starving for one true word about the place that took his child.)* …You've *been* in it. *(a whisper)* All these years I have *imagined* it — the cold, the dissolving, the *nothing* — and *built* an *engine of war* on the *imagining.* And you can simply *tell* me. *(his hands are not quite steady)* Then tell me, Returned. *All* of it. Is she — *(he stops, corrects, the father bleeding through the general)* — are they *suffering* in there? The unclaimed? Is it *pain*, or is it *sleep*, or is it the *erasure* the heresies describe? *(when you tell him true — the cold, the patience, the *forgetting* a piece at a time, oldest first; and also the *thread*, that a soul mortared while *loving* someone can leave a little of itself *snagged* on the living; and that the door, impossibly, swings *both* ways — he makes a sound you will not forget, half a sob and half a man surfacing from twenty years underwater.)* …A *thread.* *(he looks, involuntarily, at the empty chair — and does not know, cannot know, how close he is)* You're saying a soul that went into the cold *looking* at the one it loved might leave — *something* — *behind.* *(he presses his fist to his mouth)* I was *there*, Returned. At the causeway. She was *looking* at me when the cold — *(he cannot finish)* *(a long, devastating silence, and then, very low:)* …Is it possible she *followed me home?* That I have spent twenty years pouring tea for a chair that is *not* — that was never — *(he looks at you, and the whole of his terrible cause hangs on your next breath)* …*Don't* comfort me. *Don't.* I have built an *army* on *not* being comforted. Tell me only what is *true.* Could she be *here?*

nodes.speaker: Aldric Morn
nodes.text: *(Something kindles behind his grey eyes — the old fire of the cause, banked but never out.)* The Crown of Horns. *(quietly)* They say it was forged from the antlers of the first thing that ever *refused* to die when a god told it to. *(he turns his cup)* It is the *authority* of the realm of the dead, made *wearable.* Whoever holds it can *command* the architecture of the afterlife — the gates, the courts, the *Wall.* The Choir guards the *rumor* of it more jealously than the Wall itself, because the Wall is only a *prison*, and the Crown is the *key to every lock in death.* *(he meets your eyes)* What it *costs* — *(a pause)* — I will not lie to a Returned. To take it, a soul must walk *into* death *willingly*, find the Court that the living are not meant to find, and *win* it from whatever wears it now. The living do not come *back* from that road. *(and here he looks at you with a dawning, dangerous understanding)* …The *living* don't. *(softly)* But you are not *quite* living, are you. You are the *one* kind of soul that *walks into death and comes back out.* *(he sets the cup down, slowly)* That is why I *sent* for you, Returned. Not for your *sword.* Not for your *cause.* Because you are the *only* creature on the *Sword Coast* who could walk the *Crown's* road and *return* to *tell* it. *(quietly, terribly)* I have spent twenty years looking for a *door* into death that opens *both* ways. And one walked up my causeway and *sat at my table.* …So. Now you know what I *want.* The question that's *left* is whether you'll *give* it — and what *you* mean to do with the Crown, if *you're* the one who comes back *wearing* it.

nodes.speaker: Aldric Morn
nodes.text: *(He folds his hands around the cooling cup, and the kettle has gone quiet, and the empty chair sits between you like a third party to the conversation — which, though only one of you knows it, it is.)* So here we are, Returned. *(quietly)* I am an old monster with a *count* and a *kettle* and a *daughter-shaped* hole, and you are the answer to a prayer I stopped saying. I want the Wall *down.* You are the one soul who could walk the *Crown's* road and *return.* *(he meets your eyes)* I will not *force* you — I gave up the *right* to call my coercions *anything but what they are* a long time ago, and I will not *lie* to a soul that has been where my *daughter* is. *(he leans forward)* So I ask you *plainly*, the only way left worth asking: *will you walk it?* And if you do — *(his voice drops)* — whose *hand* ends up on that Crown, and what does it *do* with the *key to every lock in death?* Tell me the *shape* of you, Returned. I have shown you *mine.* It is the *ugliest* honest thing I own.

choices.text: "I'll walk it, Aldric — but not for your war, and not to crown anyone a god, you or me. I walk it to open the *appeal*: the Court hears every name in the Wall, the backlog clears, and the *seat* stays exactly as full as it already is. No new god. No burned cities. Just the door the Choir skipped, forced open at last."

*(he suspects the thread; give him the door, not the cliff)* "Before I answer the Crown — turn around, Aldric. Look at the chair. Not the memory. The *chair.* I'm Returned; I see what the living can't. She followed you home. Eithne's been sitting with you every night for twenty years. She's *here.* And what she wants — the only thing she's wanted — is for you to *stop.*"

"I'll walk it. And I'll take the Crown myself, Aldric — because someone has to, and I'd rather it were a soul that's already been emptied of the things a god gets drunk on. You want a just king of the dead? Fine. I'll be the one who frees them and then *sets the crown down.* That's the part you could never promise. I can."

"No. I've walked your causeway, Aldric, and I've seen what the *spending* does — to the spent, and to the spender. You're not freeing the dead. You're feeding the same machine a different way and calling it mercy. I won't be your key. Put down the matches, or you'll have to get past *me* to light them."

nodes.speaker: Aldric Morn
nodes.text: *(He is silent for a long, long moment. Then, slowly, he reaches across the table — not for your cup, but past it — and turns the empty chair, just slightly, so it faces the fire instead of the cold window. A small thing. An enormous thing.)* …The *appeal.* *(barely)* The seat stays *full.* No new god. *(he looks at his own hands as though seeing them clean for the first time in years)* Twenty years, Returned. Twenty years I was *certain* the only roads were *burn it* or *rule it* — *destruction* or *apotheosis* — because those were the only two a *grieving* man could *imagine*, and grief has *no* imagination, that is its *cruelty.* *(his voice shakes)* And you've named a *third.* Not *tear the Wall down.* Not *take the Judge's throne.* Just — *make the court do its job.* *Force the hearing it skipped.* *(he presses his hand flat on the table)* That is not a *war.* That is a *lawsuit.* On behalf of *ten thousand* dead. *(a sound that might be a laugh, might be a sob)* I raised a daughter who would not *lie* to be *safe.* And the way to *free* her was *never* the *fire* — it was to be as *stubbornly honest* as *she* was, and *demand* the *fairness* I spent forty years *preaching.* *(he looks at you, and the monster is, for this moment, just a father again)* I will give you *everything.* My maps, my heresies, my allies, the *Crown's* road itself. Walk it, force the appeal, and let the *first* name the Court hears be — *(his voice fails on it)* — be *Eithne Morn.* Let my honest girl get the *fair* reckoning her *father's god* owed her. *(quietly)* And I will put down the matches. Tonight. You have my *word*, and I have not given it *cleanly* in *twenty years.*

nodes.speaker: Aldric Morn
nodes.text: *(He goes white. He does not turn — not at first — because turning means *believing*, and believing is the thing he has armored against for twenty years. His hands shake on the cup.)* …Don't. *(a whisper)* Don't you *dare*, if it's not — I have *built* a *life* on her being *gone*, I cannot — *(but you are Returned, and you do not lie about the dead, and he knows it, and slowly, slowly, Aldric Morn turns in his chair and looks at the place he has set a cup every night for twenty years.)* *(He cannot see her. He will never see her — that sight is yours alone. But you can: and you watch Eithne rise from the chair, weightless and faded and *blazing*, and put her arms around her father who cannot feel them, and you watch the cold of her settle over him — and you watch Aldric Morn *shudder*, and go still, and press his hand to the air beside his own face where, though he cannot know it, his daughter's faded hand has come to rest.)* …*Eithne.* *(broken, barely)* I can't — there's just *cold*, I can't *see* — but it's — *(his voice shatters completely)* — it's the *good* cold. It's *her* cold. I'd know it — twenty years — I'd know it *anywhere.* *(he is weeping, openly, a thing you suspect he has not done since the causeway)* *(And then he says the thing she has waited twenty years to hear, and he says it because you gave him the door:)* …What do you *want*, my honest girl? Not what I've *decided* you want. What do *you* — *(he listens to the silence, and you, who can hear her, watch her say it, and you carry it to him:)* — *stop.* *(he repeats it, wondering, devastated)* She wants me to *stop.* She would rather have *faded* than have me *become* this. *(he bows his head)* …Of *course* she would. She never *once* told a lie to be *safe.* Why did I think she'd want me to *burn the world* to be *free.* *(quietly, to the cold air, to his daughter, to twenty wasted years)* …All right, Eithne. *All right.* I'll put it down. I'll put *all* of it down. *(he looks at you, wrecked and, impossibly, *lighter* than any man you have ever seen)* You did not bring me an *ally*, Returned. You brought me my *daughter's voice.* There is no *debt* large enough. Ask me for *anything.* The Crown, the cause, my *life* — it is *yours.* You gave me the only thing left I *wanted*, and I did not even know I could still *want* it.

nodes.speaker: Aldric Morn
nodes.text: *(He studies you for a long moment, and what crosses his face is recognition, and warning, and a grief that is almost *paternal.*)* …You'll *set it down.* *(quietly)* That's what you say *now.* And I *believe* you mean it — gods, I *believed it of myself*, once, when the cause was *young* and I was *only* a grieving man and not yet a *monster* with a count. *(he leans forward, urgent)* Listen to an old man who walked further down *your* exact road than you have *yet*: the Crown does not *let* you set it down. Not because it *binds* you — because it *works.* You free the dead, and it feels like *justice*, and then there is *one more* injustice you could fix if you *kept* it, and *one* more, and the road from *'I'll set it down'* to *'only I can be trusted to hold it'* is *paved* with *exactly* the *mercy* you carry now. *(he holds your gaze)* I am not telling you *no.* I am the *last* soul with the *standing* to tell you no. *(quietly)* I am telling you that if you walk that road, you must carry someone who will *take the Crown off your head by force* when you start explaining why you *can't* set it down yet. *(a thin, sad smile)* Find that someone, Returned. *Before* you walk it. *I* didn't. That is the whole reason I'm a *monster* with a *kettle* instead of a *king* — I got *stopped*, by *failure*, before I ever reached the *throne.* You may not be so *lucky* as to *fail.* *(he nods, slowly)* …But yes. I'll give you the road. Walk it your way. And may you have *better* people around you than I *did* — the kind who *love* you enough to *uncrown* you. That, in the end, is the *only* check on a *good* soul with a *god's* power. Not *law.* *Love*, with the *nerve* to *act.*

nodes.speaker: Aldric Morn
nodes.text: *(He does not reach for a weapon. He does not raise his voice. He simply sets down his cup, and looks at you with a grief so vast and so *unsurprised* that it is worse than any threat.)* …No. *(quietly)* Of course. *(he nods, slowly)* You've walked my causeway, and you've *seen* the spending, and you've decided — *rightly*, by every measure a *good* soul should use — that I am a *monster* dressed as a *mercy.* *(he meets your eyes)* You're not *wrong*, Returned. That is the *unbearable* part. You are standing in my house telling me the *truest* thing anyone has said to me in *twenty years*, and the truest thing is *no.* *(he turns his cup)* I will not *fight* you. I am *tired*, and you are the *one* soul I would not *spend*, and besides — *(a thin, exhausted smile)* — you'd *win*, and the *causeway* would lose the only thing it's *gained* this week. *(quietly)* So go. Take your *refusal* and your *mercy* and your *gentle* roads. Maybe you're *right* that there's a way down that doesn't *burn.* Maybe you'll *find* it, where I *couldn't.* *(he looks at the empty chair)* But understand: I will *still* be here. *Pouring* the cup. *Keeping* the count. Because *somebody* has to want that Wall down *enough* to do the *unforgivable* — and until *you* prove the *gentle* road is *real*, the *unforgivable* is the *only* road I *have.* *(he picks the cup back up)* *Prove* me *wrong*, Returned. *Please.* It is the one defeat I have *prayed* for. Now — the door's behind you. Wessa will see you out. And — *(very quietly)* — if you *do* find the gentle road… *come back* and *tell* me. Even an old monster would like to put the *matches* down. I just won't do it *blind.*
